questions.question: Why is service discovery not the same thing as placement?
options: Discovery finds candidate endpoints, while placement decides which healthy endpoint should receive this request now.
Discovery only works in databases.
Placement only matters for static assets.
They are exactly the same concept in all systems.
questions.explanation: A registry alone does not encode the routing decision policy.

questions.question: Why do many systems cache endpoint data locally in clients or proxies?
options: To keep request-path latency low and avoid making the registry a hot dependency.
To guarantee that routing is always perfectly fresh.
To remove any need for health checks.
To ensure all callers hit the same endpoint.
questions.explanation: Local caches improve resilience and latency, at the cost of bounded staleness.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with client-side discovery?
options: It can reduce central proxy cost, but routing logic and config handling spread across many callers.
It forces every request through a global proxy.
It guarantees no stale endpoint data.
It eliminates rollout concerns.
questions.explanation: The upside is fewer middle hops; the downside is distributed policy complexity.

questions.question: Which signal best detects dangerous config staleness in the fleet?
options: Version skew and stale-cache age across clients, proxies, or POPs.
Only total CPU on one registry node.
The size of a README file.
Average code comment length.
questions.explanation: Version drift directly reveals inconsistent routing state.

questions.question: Why can locality-only placement be risky?
options: It may send traffic to the nearest endpoints even when those endpoints are overloaded or unhealthy.
It prevents any request from being routed.
It is incompatible with health checks.
It only matters in single-region systems.
questions.explanation: Low latency is not enough if the chosen endpoint cannot safely serve the traffic.

questions.question: What is a strong mitigation when a recovered endpoint starts getting stampeded immediately?
options: Ramp traffic gradually with weighting, jitter, and concurrency guards.
Disable all health signals.
Route all callers to the recovered endpoint at once.
Shorten every client timeout to zero.
questions.explanation: Recovery needs controlled ramp-up, not instant full load.

questions.question: What is the clearest reason to keep the data plane working during control-plane trouble?
options: A service registry or policy distributor should not become a request-path single point of failure.
It is easier to draw on a whiteboard.
It avoids any need for observability.
It makes endpoint health irrelevant.
questions.explanation: Cached routing state keeps the data plane resilient when central systems degrade.

questions.question: What is the best first clarification when asked to design service discovery?
options: Ask whether the challenge is endpoint lookup only, or also capacity-aware placement and rollout control.
Assume the registry solves everything.
Avoid asking about update frequency.
Start by choosing a specific vendor product.
questions.explanation: The real design difficulty often sits in placement policy, not naming a registry.
